question:
B-3. Prove that if \( \alpha \) is a real number such that
\[
\cos \pi \alpha=1 / 3,
\]
then \( \alpha \) is irrational. (The angle \( \pi \alpha \) is in radians.)
solution:
B-3.
If \( \alpha=r / s \) with \( r \) and \( s \) integers and \( s>0 \), then \( \cos (n \pi \alpha) \) takes on at most \( 2 s \) distinct values for integral choices of \( n \). When \( \cos \pi \alpha=1 / 3 \), the formula \( \cos 2 \theta=2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1 \) and mathematical induction can be used to show that
\[
\cos \left(2^{m} \pi \alpha\right)=t / 3^{2^{m}} \quad \quad[m=1,2,3, \cdots]
\]
with \( t \) an integer not divisible by 3 , and hence that these cosines form an infinite set of distinct values. Thus \( \alpha \) is irrational.

descriptive_long.question:
B-3. Prove that if \( anglevar \) is a real number such that
\[
\cos \pi anglevar=1 / 3,
\]
then \( anglevar \) is irrational. (The angle \( \pi anglevar \) is in radians.)
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3.
If \( anglevar=intnumer / intdenom \) with \( intnumer \) and \( intdenom \) integers and \( intdenom>0 \), then \( \cos (indexint \pi anglevar) \) takes on at most \( 2 intdenom \) distinct values for integral choices of \( indexint \). When \( \cos \pi anglevar=1 / 3 \), the formula \( \cos 2 auxangle=2 \cos ^{2} auxangle-1 \) and mathematical induction can be used to show that
\[
\cos \left(2^{powindex} \pi anglevar\right)=intconst / 3^{2^{powindex}} \quad \quad[powindex=1,2,3, \cdots]
\]
with \( intconst \) an integer not divisible by 3 , and hence that these cosines form an infinite set of distinct values. Thus \( anglevar \) is irrational.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-3. Prove that if \( marmalade \) is a real number such that
\[
\cos \pi marmalade=1 / 3,
\]
then marmalade is irrational. (The angle \( \pi marmalade \) is in radians.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3.
If \( marmalade=pinecone / driftwood \) with \( pinecone \) and \( driftwood \) integers and \( driftwood>0 \), then \( \cos (rainstorm \pi marmalade) \) takes on at most \( 2 driftwood \) distinct values for integral choices of \( rainstorm \). When \( \cos \pi marmalade=1 / 3 \), the formula \( \cos 2 wildberry=2 \cos ^{2} wildberry-1 \) and mathematical induction can be used to show that
\[
\cos \left(2^{stonework} \pi marmalade\right)=clifftops / 3^{2^{stonework}} \quad \quad[stonework=1,2,3, \cdots]
\]
with \( clifftops \) an integer not divisible by 3 , and hence that these cosines form an infinite set of distinct values. Thus \( marmalade \) is irrational.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-3. Prove that if \( omegasymbol \) is a real number such that
\[
\cos \pi omegasymbol=1 / 3,
\]
then \( omegasymbol \) is irrational. (The angle \( \pi omegasymbol \) is in radians.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3.
If \( omegasymbol=irrational / numerator \) with \( irrational \) and \( numerator \) integers and \( numerator>0 \), then \( \cos (fractional \pi omegasymbol) \) takes on at most \( 2 numerator \) distinct values for integral choices of \( fractional \). When \( \cos \pi omegasymbol=1 / 3 \), the formula \( \cos 2 straightline=2 \cos ^{2} straightline-1 \) and mathematical induction can be used to show that
\[
\cos \left(2^{constantvalue} \pi omegasymbol\right)=divisible / 3^{2^{constantvalue}} \quad \quad[constantvalue=1,2,3, \cdots]
\]
with \( divisible \) an integer not divisible by 3 , and hence that these cosines form an infinite set of distinct values. Thus \( omegasymbol \) is irrational.

garbled_string.question:
B-3. Prove that if \( \qzxwvtnp \) is a real number such that
\[
\cos \pi \qzxwvtnp = 1 / 3,
\]
then \( \qzxwvtnp \) is irrational. (The angle \( \pi \qzxwvtnp \) is in radians.)
garbled_string.solution:
B-3.
If \( \qzxwvtnp = qprnjdks / xlmvczbt \) with \( qprnjdks \) and \( xlmvczbt \) integers and \( xlmvczbt>0 \), then \( \cos ( zvy rhgpl \pi \qzxwvtnp ) \) takes on at most \( 2 xlmvczbt \) distinct values for integral choices of \( zvyrhgpl \). When \( \cos \pi \qzxwvtnp = 1 / 3 \), the formula \( \cos 2 \hjgrksla = 2 \cos ^{2} \hjgrksla - 1 \) and mathematical induction can be used to show that
\[
\cos \left(2^{ kdfsghtr } \pi \qzxwvtnp \right)= skvnjmwr / 3^{ 2^{ kdfsghtr } } \quad \quad[ kdfsghtr = 1,2,3, \cdots]
\]
with \( skvnjmwr \) an integer not divisible by 3, and hence that these cosines form an infinite set of distinct values. Thus \( \qzxwvtnp \) is irrational.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \alpha  be a real number such that  
  cos (\pi  \alpha )=2/5.

(a)  Prove that \alpha  is irrational.

(b)  Let p,s be positive integers with gcd(p,s)=1.  
 Determine exactly for which values of s the number  

  cos(\pi  p/s)

 is rational, and for every such s list all possible rational values of the cosine.

(c)  Using your answer to (b) together with the special value cos (\pi  \alpha )=2/5, show that k \alpha  is irrational for every non-zero integer k.  
(Throughout, angles are measured in radians.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  \zeta _n:=e^{2\pi  i/n}.  
For m\geq 0 let T_m be the Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind,
 T_0(x)=1, T_1(x)=x, T_{m+1}(x)=2xT_m(x)-T_{m-1}(x);  
they satisfy T_m(cos \theta )=cos(m \theta ) and T_m\in \mathbb{Z}[x].

-------------------------------------------------
(a) Irrationality of \alpha 

Assume, for contradiction, that \alpha =p/q with gcd(p,q)=1 and q>0.  
Put  

 \beta :=2 cos(\pi  \alpha )=4/5\in \mathbb{Q}. (1)

Write p=2^{\varepsilon }p_0 with \varepsilon \in {0,1} and p_0 odd.

*  Case \varepsilon =0 (p odd).  
 Then e^{i\pi  \alpha }=\zeta _{2q}^{\,p} is a primitive 2q-th root of unity, so let n:=2q.  
 Because \beta =\zeta _n^{\,p}+\zeta _n^{-p}, we have \beta \in \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}).  
 We now show that \beta  actually generates the whole real cyclotomic subfield.

  Lemma 1.  If gcd(t,n)=1, then \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t} is a primitive element of \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}); in particular  
   \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t}) = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}).  

  Proof.  Let \sigma _t:\zeta _n\mapsto \zeta _n^{\,t}.  Since gcd(t,n)=1, \sigma _t is an automorphism of \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n).  Hence  
   \sigma _t(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}) = \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t}.  
  Thus \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t} is Galois-conjugate to \zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}, and both elements therefore generate the same fixed field of complex conjugation, namely the real subfield. \blacksquare 

 Applying Lemma 1 with t=p gives  
  [\mathbb{Q}(\beta ):\mathbb{Q}]=[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}):\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi (n)/2   (n>2). (2)

 Because \beta \in \mathbb{Q}, we must have \varphi (n)/2=1, i.e. \varphi (n)=2.  The positive integers n with \varphi (n)=2 are n=3,4,6.  Here n=2q is even and \geq 4, hence n\in {4,6} giving q\in {2,3}.  
  - If q=2 then \alpha  is a half-integer and cos(\pi  \alpha )\in {\pm 1,0}.  
  - If q=3 then \alpha =p/3 with p odd and cos(\pi  \alpha )=\pm 1/2.  
 Neither possibility equals 2/5, contradicting (1).

*  Case \varepsilon =1 (p even).  
 Write p=2p_0 (p_0 odd); now q is odd.  Then e^{i\pi  \alpha }=\zeta _q^{\,p_0} is primitive, so put n:=q.  
 Exactly as above, \beta  generates \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}) and (2) holds.  With n odd, \varphi (n)=2 gives n=3 and q=3.  Then \alpha =2p_0/3 and cos(\pi  \alpha )=cos(2\pi  p_0/3)=\pm 1/2, again contradicting (1).

Both cases are impossible; hence \alpha  is irrational.

-------------------------------------------------
(b) Rational angles yielding rational cosines

Let \theta :=p/s with gcd(p,s)=1, s>0, and set  

 \gamma :=2 cos(\pi  p/s)=\zeta _{2s}^{\,p}+\zeta _{2s}^{-p}. (3)

Write p=2^{\varepsilon }p_0 with \varepsilon \in {0,1} and p_0 odd, and define  

 m:=  { 2s if \varepsilon =0 (p odd),  
     s  if \varepsilon =1 (p even).     (4)

If \varepsilon =1 then s is necessarily odd, so \zeta _{2s}^{\,p} becomes a primitive m-th root of unity; if \varepsilon =0 the primitivity is evident.  Thus \gamma =\zeta _m^{\,p_0}+\zeta _m^{-p_0} with gcd(p_0,m)=1.

By Lemma 1, \gamma  is a primitive element of the real cyclotomic subfield \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _m+\zeta _m^{-1}).  Consequently  

 [\mathbb{Q}(\gamma ):\mathbb{Q}]= { 1 if m\leq 2,  
       \varphi (m)/2 if m\geq 3.    (5)

Therefore \gamma \in \mathbb{Q} exactly when m\leq 2 or \varphi (m)=2.  As before \varphi (m)=2 \Leftrightarrow  m\in {3,4,6}.  Translating back to s via (4) gives

 m=1,2 \Rightarrow  s=1  \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p)=\pm 1;  
 m=3  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =1, s=3 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/3)=\pm 1/2;  
 m=4  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =0, s=2 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/2)=0;  
 m=6  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =0, s=3 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/3)=\pm 1/2.

Hence

 s\in {1,2,3} and cos(\pi  p/s)\in {1,-1,0,1/2,-1/2}. (6)

Conversely each value in (6) is achieved for some p coprime to the corresponding s (for instance p\equiv 1 mod 2,3), so the classification is complete.

-------------------------------------------------
(c) Every non-zero integral multiple of \alpha  is irrational

For any k\in \mathbb{Z} we have  

 cos(k \pi  \alpha )=T_{|k|}(cos \pi  \alpha )=T_{|k|}(2/5)\in \mathbb{Q}, (7)

using evenness of cosine to reduce to |k|.  Assume that k\alpha =r/s with k\neq 0, gcd(r,s)=1, s>0.  
Then cos(k \pi  \alpha )=cos(\pi  r/s) is rational, so by (b)

 s\in {1,2,3} and cos(\pi  r/s)\in {\pm 1,0,\pm 1/2}. (8)

On the other hand expand T_{|k|}:

 T_{|k|}(x)=\sum _{j=0}^{\lfloor |k|/2\rfloor } a_{k,j}\,x^{|k|-2j}, a_{k,j}\in \mathbb{Z}, a_{k,0}=2^{|k|-1} (|k|\geq 1).

Plugging x=2/5 gives  

 5^{|k|}T_{|k|}(2/5)=2^{2|k|-1}+5\cdot M, M\in \mathbb{Z}. (9)

The leading term 2^{2|k|-1} is not divisible by 5, whereas every other term contains a factor 5.  Thus the reduced fraction T_{|k|}(2/5) has denominator exactly 5^{|k|}.  Consequently

 T_{|k|}(2/5)\notin {\pm 1,0,\pm 1/2},  (|k|\geq 1). (10)

This contradicts (8).  Therefore no non-zero integral multiple of \alpha  can be rational; i.e. k \alpha \notin \mathbb{Q} for all k\neq 0.

-------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
The original problem merely asked to show that a single specific value α with
cos(π α)=1/3 is irrational, using elementary doubling identities.  The present
variant introduces three major layers of additional complexity:                

1.  Cyclotomic‐field machinery and Euler’s totient function are required to
    link rational angles to the degree of roots of unity, something entirely
    absent from the original.                                                  

2.  Part (b) demands a complete classification of all rational angles whose
    cosine is rational, forcing the solver to solve φ(n)=2 and to understand
    why only n∈{3,4,6} occur; this pushes the problem well beyond simple
    trigonometric identities into algebraic-number theory and group theory.    

3.  Part (c) couples the algebraic classification with explicit evaluations of
    Chebyshev polynomials, requiring knowledge of their arithmetic properties
    (denominators that are powers of 5) to exclude every historically allowed
    rational value, thereby ruling out rational multiples of α.                

These added requirements make the problem significantly harder: the solver
must blend elementary trigonometry with cyclotomic fields, degree arguments,
and polynomial arithmetic, whereas the original solution needed only an
induction on a double-angle formula.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let \alpha  be a real number such that  
  cos (\pi  \alpha )=2/5.

(a)  Prove that \alpha  is irrational.

(b)  Let p,s be positive integers with gcd(p,s)=1.  
 Determine exactly for which values of s the number  

  cos(\pi  p/s)

 is rational, and for every such s list all possible rational values of the cosine.

(c)  Using your answer to (b) together with the special value cos (\pi  \alpha )=2/5, show that k \alpha  is irrational for every non-zero integer k.  
(Throughout, angles are measured in radians.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  \zeta _n:=e^{2\pi  i/n}.  
For m\geq 0 let T_m be the Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind,
 T_0(x)=1, T_1(x)=x, T_{m+1}(x)=2xT_m(x)-T_{m-1}(x);  
they satisfy T_m(cos \theta )=cos(m \theta ) and T_m\in \mathbb{Z}[x].

-------------------------------------------------
(a) Irrationality of \alpha 

Assume, for contradiction, that \alpha =p/q with gcd(p,q)=1 and q>0.  
Put  

 \beta :=2 cos(\pi  \alpha )=4/5\in \mathbb{Q}. (1)

Write p=2^{\varepsilon }p_0 with \varepsilon \in {0,1} and p_0 odd.

*  Case \varepsilon =0 (p odd).  
 Then e^{i\pi  \alpha }=\zeta _{2q}^{\,p} is a primitive 2q-th root of unity, so let n:=2q.  
 Because \beta =\zeta _n^{\,p}+\zeta _n^{-p}, we have \beta \in \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}).  
 We now show that \beta  actually generates the whole real cyclotomic subfield.

  Lemma 1.  If gcd(t,n)=1, then \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t} is a primitive element of \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}); in particular  
   \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t}) = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}).  

  Proof.  Let \sigma _t:\zeta _n\mapsto \zeta _n^{\,t}.  Since gcd(t,n)=1, \sigma _t is an automorphism of \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n).  Hence  
   \sigma _t(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}) = \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t}.  
  Thus \zeta _n^{\,t}+\zeta _n^{-t} is Galois-conjugate to \zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}, and both elements therefore generate the same fixed field of complex conjugation, namely the real subfield. \blacksquare 

 Applying Lemma 1 with t=p gives  
  [\mathbb{Q}(\beta ):\mathbb{Q}]=[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}):\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi (n)/2   (n>2). (2)

 Because \beta \in \mathbb{Q}, we must have \varphi (n)/2=1, i.e. \varphi (n)=2.  The positive integers n with \varphi (n)=2 are n=3,4,6.  Here n=2q is even and \geq 4, hence n\in {4,6} giving q\in {2,3}.  
  - If q=2 then \alpha  is a half-integer and cos(\pi  \alpha )\in {\pm 1,0}.  
  - If q=3 then \alpha =p/3 with p odd and cos(\pi  \alpha )=\pm 1/2.  
 Neither possibility equals 2/5, contradicting (1).

*  Case \varepsilon =1 (p even).  
 Write p=2p_0 (p_0 odd); now q is odd.  Then e^{i\pi  \alpha }=\zeta _q^{\,p_0} is primitive, so put n:=q.  
 Exactly as above, \beta  generates \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _n+\zeta _n^{-1}) and (2) holds.  With n odd, \varphi (n)=2 gives n=3 and q=3.  Then \alpha =2p_0/3 and cos(\pi  \alpha )=cos(2\pi  p_0/3)=\pm 1/2, again contradicting (1).

Both cases are impossible; hence \alpha  is irrational.

-------------------------------------------------
(b) Rational angles yielding rational cosines

Let \theta :=p/s with gcd(p,s)=1, s>0, and set  

 \gamma :=2 cos(\pi  p/s)=\zeta _{2s}^{\,p}+\zeta _{2s}^{-p}. (3)

Write p=2^{\varepsilon }p_0 with \varepsilon \in {0,1} and p_0 odd, and define  

 m:=  { 2s if \varepsilon =0 (p odd),  
     s  if \varepsilon =1 (p even).     (4)

If \varepsilon =1 then s is necessarily odd, so \zeta _{2s}^{\,p} becomes a primitive m-th root of unity; if \varepsilon =0 the primitivity is evident.  Thus \gamma =\zeta _m^{\,p_0}+\zeta _m^{-p_0} with gcd(p_0,m)=1.

By Lemma 1, \gamma  is a primitive element of the real cyclotomic subfield \mathbb{Q}(\zeta _m+\zeta _m^{-1}).  Consequently  

 [\mathbb{Q}(\gamma ):\mathbb{Q}]= { 1 if m\leq 2,  
       \varphi (m)/2 if m\geq 3.    (5)

Therefore \gamma \in \mathbb{Q} exactly when m\leq 2 or \varphi (m)=2.  As before \varphi (m)=2 \Leftrightarrow  m\in {3,4,6}.  Translating back to s via (4) gives

 m=1,2 \Rightarrow  s=1  \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p)=\pm 1;  
 m=3  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =1, s=3 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/3)=\pm 1/2;  
 m=4  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =0, s=2 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/2)=0;  
 m=6  \Rightarrow  \varepsilon =0, s=3 \Rightarrow  cos(\pi  p/3)=\pm 1/2.

Hence

 s\in {1,2,3} and cos(\pi  p/s)\in {1,-1,0,1/2,-1/2}. (6)

Conversely each value in (6) is achieved for some p coprime to the corresponding s (for instance p\equiv 1 mod 2,3), so the classification is complete.

-------------------------------------------------
(c) Every non-zero integral multiple of \alpha  is irrational

For any k\in \mathbb{Z} we have  

 cos(k \pi  \alpha )=T_{|k|}(cos \pi  \alpha )=T_{|k|}(2/5)\in \mathbb{Q}, (7)

using evenness of cosine to reduce to |k|.  Assume that k\alpha =r/s with k\neq 0, gcd(r,s)=1, s>0.  
Then cos(k \pi  \alpha )=cos(\pi  r/s) is rational, so by (b)

 s\in {1,2,3} and cos(\pi  r/s)\in {\pm 1,0,\pm 1/2}. (8)

On the other hand expand T_{|k|}:

 T_{|k|}(x)=\sum _{j=0}^{\lfloor |k|/2\rfloor } a_{k,j}\,x^{|k|-2j}, a_{k,j}\in \mathbb{Z}, a_{k,0}=2^{|k|-1} (|k|\geq 1).

Plugging x=2/5 gives  

 5^{|k|}T_{|k|}(2/5)=2^{2|k|-1}+5\cdot M, M\in \mathbb{Z}. (9)

The leading term 2^{2|k|-1} is not divisible by 5, whereas every other term contains a factor 5.  Thus the reduced fraction T_{|k|}(2/5) has denominator exactly 5^{|k|}.  Consequently

 T_{|k|}(2/5)\notin {\pm 1,0,\pm 1/2},  (|k|\geq 1). (10)

This contradicts (8).  Therefore no non-zero integral multiple of \alpha  can be rational; i.e. k \alpha \notin \mathbb{Q} for all k\neq 0.

-------------------------------------------------
